coredata.dc:title: Novel carbon dots for zinc sensing from Campomanesia phaea
coredata.dc:creator: da Silva Júnior, Afonso Henrique
Lusitâneo Pier Macuvele, Domingos
Gracher Riella, Humberto
Soares, Cíntia
Padoin, Natan
coredata.dc:description: Highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized from cambuci juice (Campomanesia phaea (O. Berg) Landrum) and successfully used for zinc (Zn2+) sensing based on fluorescence quenching. The CDs were synthesized at different pH, using NaOH and NH4OH as pH adjusters. The CDs synthesized were characterized by Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), X-Ray Diffraction (XRD), Raman spectroscopy, Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR), Zeta potential, UV–Vis, and fluorescence spectroscopy. The CDs synthesized from juice with pH 7 and adjusted by NH4OH presented a high sensitivity to Zn2+ and a higher quantum yield (21.26%). The minimum limit of detection (LOD) was 5.4 µм. The linear range (LR) is between 25 and 125 µм. The sensor exhibits great potential for applications as a zinc detector for biological and environmental systems.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Cambuci juice
Campomanesia phaea
Fluorescent nanomaterial
Zinc ion detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Selective detection of Zn2+ ions with CDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Coordenação de Aperfeiçoamento de Pessoal de Nível Superior

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–Vis absorption spectra - inset shows image of the CDs under sunlight (1) and UV irradiation (2). (b) FTIR. (c) TEM micrograph of CDs. (d) Diameter distribution of CDs.
(a) Zeta potential for all conditions with a pH range between 3 and 11. (b) XRD profile for all conditions. (c) Raman spectra of the CDs-2.5, Na-CDs-5, and NH-CDs-7.

(a) Fluorescence spectrum at different concentrations of zinc ion (λEX = 360 nm). The dependence of the value (F0-F)/F0 by the zinc concentration in the range from: (b) 0 to 500 μм and (c) 0 to 125 μм. (d) Fluorescence stability for NH-CDs-7 as a function of time over seven days being irradiated under UV light.

head.ce:title: Novel carbon dots for zinc sensing from Campomanesia phaea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratory of Materials and Scientific Computing (LabMAC), Department of Chemical and Food Engineering, Federal University of Santa Catarina, Florianópolis, SC, Brazil

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Laboratory of Materials and Scientific Computing (LabMAC)
Department of Chemical and Food Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Laboratory of Materials and Scientific Computing (LabMAC), Department of Chemical and Food Engineering, Federal University of Santa Catarina, Florianópolis, SC, Brazil

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Science, Engineering, Technology and Mathematics, University of Rovuma, Lichinga, Mozambique

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Science, Engineering, Technology and Mathematics

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Natural Science and Mathematics, University of Rovuma-Extension of Niassa (Extinct Pedagógica University of Mozambique), Lichinga, Mozambique

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized from cambuci juice (Campomanesia phaea (O. Berg) Landrum) and successfully used for zinc (Zn2+) sensing based on fluorescence quenching. The CDs were synthesized at different pH, using NaOH and NH4OH as pH adjusters. The CDs synthesized were characterized by Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), X-Ray Diffraction (XRD), Raman spectroscopy, Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR), Zeta potential, UV–Vis, and fluorescence spectroscopy. The CDs synthesized from juice with pH 7 and adjusted by NH4OH presented a high sensitivity to Zn2+ and a higher quantum yield (21.26%). The minimum limit of detection (LOD) was 5.4 µм. The linear range (LR) is between 25 and 125 µм. The sensor exhibits great potential for applications as a zinc detector for biological and environmental systems.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are a new class of materials with many advantages, such as low cytotoxicity, stable photoluminescence, good biocompatibility, and easy functionalization [1]. Due to these properties, CDs have potential applications in fluorescence cell imaging, catalysis, photovoltaic devices, optics, and sensors of ions [2]. These materials are conventionally obtained from analytical grade reagents. However, due to environmental issues, recent studies have dedicated their efforts to obtain nanoparticles from green routes. CDs are synthesized from various methods, namely chemical oxidation, ultrasonication, microwave, hydrothermal, and others.

Zn2+ is an essential micronutrient in biological routes [3]. The conventional methods for detection, namely Atomic Absorption Spectrometry (AAS), Neutron Activation Analysis (NAA), and spectrophotometry using UV–Vis, have some disadvantages, such as lack of selectivity, interferents, and expensive reagents. There are only a few studies on the detection of zinc ions by carbon dots. Cambuci, C. phaea, is a native fruit from Brazilian Atlantic Forest. Phytochemistry studies revealed that this fruit is composed mainly by pectin and phenolic compounds. Therefore, this study presents the novel application of cambuci fruit juice (Campomanesia phaea (O. Berg) Landrum) as a precursor of CDs for zinc ion selective detection.

The synthesis of the CDs was based on the methodology of Arumugam and Kim (2018) [4] with modifications. In this work, seven syntheses were made: one was performed without modification of fruit extract pH (2.5), and the other six at pH 5, 7, and 9. NH4OH and NaOH were used in order to modify the juice extract pH. All experiments were carried out at 190 °C during 6 h (Table 1 ). Firstly, the fruits were cleaned with distilled water to remove some impurities, and then they were crushed to obtain a paste. In an autoclave, 15 g of fruit paste and 15 g of distilled water were added. Then, the pH was corrected with two bases: sodium hydroxide and ammonium hydroxide. The time and temperature variables were kept constant in all experiments. After carbonization, the purification was performed through centrifugation and filtration on 0.22 μm cellulose acetate membranes. Finally, the supernatant was stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for further characterizations.

In a tube, 500 μL of CDs were diluted in 2 mL of distilled water along with 1 mL of buffer solution for the respective material, except for the in natura test that remained at the same pH after carbonization (Table S1). 100 μL (200 μм) of different metallic solutions were added in order to assess the selectivity of the sensor and read the fluorescence spectra with excitation at the wavelength of 360 nm. Thus, the attenuation of fluorescence was verified only for the Zn2+ ion in that wavelength. Subsequently, tests were conducted with different concentrations of the solution.

The material exhibits a brownish color in sunlight (Fig. 1 (a-1)). However, it was verified the cyan blue fluorescence color under UV light (Fig. 1(a-2)). The blue fluorescence color of CDs from some fruit juices was observed in other studies, which attributed this color matrix to the functional groups present on the surfaces of the carbon dots [5]. Fig. 1(a) shows the absorption spectra in the UV–Vis region for all experiments. All samples presented characteristic peaks in wavelengths between 270 and 283 nm, corresponding to the transitions n–π* from the band C = O. Table S2 shows the performance values of all CDs synthesized in different conditions compared to other studies. The results showed that the CDs obtained adjusting pH with NH4OH showed high QY values (19.55 – 21.26%).

Fig. 1(b) shows the FTIR spectra for all the CDs synthesized. The samples obtained from the juice extract with pH adjusted by NH4OH presented greater intensity in terms of C − N (1552 cm−1). The CDs obtained from juice treated by NaOH presented an intense band at 1406 cm−1, corresponding C − O − C group. These vibration modes, including groups with the presence of oxygen, give the material a high dispersibility in water without needing additional surface adaptation [6]. Fig. 1(c-d) shows the TEM micrographs of CDs obtained from in natura juice without pH adjustment. The CDs have an average diameter of 3.77 nm. Fig. 2 (a) shows the Zeta potential of all samples. The results show that the CDs present total negative charges. This fact is correlated to the presence of electronegative groups of atoms [7].

Fig. 2(b) shows the XRD profile of all carbon dots synthesized. All the samples presented a sharp peak at 2θ = 22.5°, which is attributed to Bragg’s flexion C(002) and the characteristic of carbonaceous material with amorphous nature. The interlayer distance was approximately 0.39 nm. Fig. 2(c) shows the Raman spectra for the tests that obtained the highest quantum yield for the two bases used and for the material without pH adjustment. The carbon dots present two characteristic bands (1379 cm−1 and 1596 cm−1) below 2000 cm−1. These band values can be explained by their graphical nature. Figure S1 shows a mechanism proposal of CDs formation. Figures S2-S3 shows the emission spectra of CDs produced by the hydrothermal process in all conditions. The fluorescence spectra were excited between the wavelengths of 260–380 nm. When changing the excitation wavelength from 260 nm to 360 nm, it was observed a gradual increase in the luminescent intensity of the curves.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective detection of Zn2+ ions with CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Figures S4-S5 shows the F/F0 ratio for each ion in the respective material. Only NH-CDs-7 presented the selectivity for the zinc ion in comparison to other metals on excitation at 360 nm, presenting a possible sensor alternative for Zn2+. Fig. 3 (a) shows that the concentration of Zn2+ (from 0 to 500 μм) increases the peak at 448 nm showing a gradual reduction in the fluorescence curve of the material. Thus, the result confirmed that the zinc ion induces the absence of fluorescence of carbon dots, which can be attributed to the coordination of proximity between the metal and the functional groups present in the material in this condition [8].

Fig. 3(b) shows a good linear relationship in the ranges between 0 and 125 μм, 125 to 200 μм, and 200 to 500 μм. In the range between 0 and 125 μм, it presented a coefficient of determination (R2) equal to 0.9978, being described by (F0-F)/F0 = 0.0006[Zn2+] – 0.0017, as shown in Fig. 3(c). The LOD was calculated by Equation 4 (Supporting Information) and evaluated at 5.4 μм as the minimum value obtained for detection. As noted in Table S3, we summarize previous works that used CDs to detect Zn2+ ions. As shown in Fig. 3(d), the intensity of the material varied slightly over seven consecutive days, suggesting that NH-CDs-7 are stable in the solution and presenting one more advantage to the material synthesized from the cambuci fruit.

In summary, this paper reported for the first time an easy, rapid, and synthesis of CDs from cambuci fruit by a green hydrothermal method. The fluorescent intensity was affected by the type of juice pH adjuster, where, the carbon dots synthesized with ammonium hydroxide showed high quantum yield (21.26%) compared to NaOH treated one. The NH-CDs-7 emitted a maximum fluorescence intensity at 448 nm (λEX = 360 nm). Also, it has excellent luminescent stability, which was sensitively and selectively the Zn2+. The sensor was based on the quenching of fluorescence at a wavelength of 360 nm and had a minimum detection limit of 5.4 µм. Therefore, it is a promising material in the detection of zinc ions in biological and environmental matrices.

Afonso Henrique da Silva Júnior: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Investigation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Domingos Lusitâneo Pier Macuvele: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Humberto Gracher Riella: Supervision, Resources, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Cíntia Soares: Supervision, Resources, Methodology, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Natan Padoin: Supervision, Resources, Methodology, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Council for the Coordination for the Improvement of Higher Education Personnel (CAPES, Brazil). We are grateful to the LAMEB/UFSC, LCME/UFSC, EQA/UFSC Analysis Center, and LINDEN/UFSC for providing their infrastructure to conduct the experimental tests and characterizations.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2020.128813.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Fig. 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ultraviolet light-driven controllable doping of graphene quantum dots with tunable emission wavelength for fluorescence bio-imaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Wang, Z. Liu, B. Gu, B. Gao, T. Wang, X. Zheng, G. Wang, Q. Guo, D. Chen, Ultraviolet light-driven controllable doping of graphene quantum dots with tunable emission wavelength for fluorescence bio-imaging, Mater. Lett. 266 (2020) 127468. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2020.127468.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hydrothermal treatment of red lentils for the synthesis of fluorescent carbon quantum dots and its application for sensing Fe3+

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z.M.S.H. Khan, R.S. Rahman, Shumaila, S. Islam, M. Zulfequar, Hydrothermal treatment of red lentils for the synthesis of fluorescent carbon quantum dots and its application for sensing Fe3+, Opt. Mater. (Amst). 91 (2019) 386–395. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.optmat.2019.03.054.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hemoglobin-incorporated iron quantum clusters as a novel fluorometric and colorimetric probe for sensing and cellular imaging of Zn(II) and cysteine

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Hashemi, Z. Vaezi, M. Sedghi, H. Naderi-Manesh, Hemoglobin-incorporated iron quantum clusters as a novel fluorometric and colorimetric probe for sensing and cellular imaging of Zn(II) and cysteine, Microchim. Acta. 185 (2018). https://doi.org/10.1007/s00604-017-2600-x.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis of carbon quantum dots from Broccoli and their ability to detect silver ions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Arumugam, J. Kim, Synthesis of carbon quantum dots from Broccoli and their ability to detect silver ions, Mater. Lett. 219 (2018) 37–40. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2018.02.043.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Confined synthesis of phosphorus, nitrogen co-doped carbon dots with green luminescence and anion recognition performance

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Yang, Y. Zhang, W. Liu, J. Gao, Y. Zheng, Confined synthesis of phosphorus, nitrogen co-doped carbon dots with green luminescence and anion recognition performance, Polyhedron. 171 (2019) 389–395. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.poly.2019.07.040.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Liu, L. Ding, L. Chen, Y. Chen, T. Zhou, H. Li, Y. Xu, L. Zhao, N. Huang, A facile, green synthesis of biomass carbon dots coupled with molecularly imprinted polymers for highly selective detection of oxytetracycline, J. Ind. Eng. Chem. 69 (2019) 455–463. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jiec.2018.10.007.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Eco-friendly synthesis of cuprizone-functionalized luminescent carbon dots and application as a sensor for the determination of copper(ii) in wastewater

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: V.A. Moreira, W. Toito Suarez, M. De Oliveira Krambeck Franco, F.F. Gambarra Neto, Eco-friendly synthesis of cuprizone-functionalized luminescent carbon dots and application as a sensor for the determination of copper(ii) in wastewater, Anal. Methods. 10 (2018) 4570–4578. https://doi.org/10.1039/c8ay00928g.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hydrothermal synthesis to water-stable luminescent carbon dots from Acerola fruit for photoluminescent composites preparation and its application as sensors

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Carvalho, L.R. Santos, J.C. Germino, A.J. Terezo, J.A. Moreto, F.J. Quites, R.G. Freitas, Hydrothermal Synthesis to Water-stable Luminescent Carbon Dots from Acerola Fruit for Photoluminescent Composites Preparation and its Application as Sensors, Mater. Res. 22 (2019). https://doi.org/10.1590/1980-5373-MR-2018-0920.
